title: Replace hand-coded checkout form with schema-driven Zod+React Hook Form and async coupon validation
business_context: A DTC e-commerce checkout (180k orders/month) has a 600-line React checkout form with ad-hoc useState validation. Coupon codes are validated only on submit, so 11% of users hit a full-page error after entering card details. Support gets ~400 tickets/week about 'invalid coupon at the end'. We want field-level async coupon validation and a reusable schema.
constraints: Coupon validation must call the pricing service (p95 220ms) without blocking other fields
Must keep the existing /api/checkout POST contract (field names unchanged) for the native apps still on v1
Form must work for 40k concurrent shoppers during flash sales without hammering the coupon endpoint (debounce + dedupe)
Backward compat: a feature flag must let us roll back to the legacy form per-region
PCI: card fields stay in the existing iframe; we only own address/coupon/shipping
No added bundle weight over 18KB gzip

before_state.description: Monolithic form with imperative validation; coupon checked only at submit causing late failures.

issues: Coupon only validated at submit, after card entry -> 11% late failures
No debounce: a fix would spam the pricing service
errors object is untyped and drifts from inputs
email check is naive (includes '@')

reasoning: Naive approach: keep useState but move the coupon fetch into an onBlur handler. Rejected: onBlur still fires one request per keystroke-pause with no dedupe, and during a 40k-concurrent flash sale that is a thundering herd on the pricing service; also it doesn't fix the untyped error drift. Chosen path: define a Zod schema as the single source of truth (data layer), drive React Hook Form from it (UI), and implement coupon as an async superRefine backed by a debounced+deduped query (async layer). UI: RHF renders field errors inline as the user types; API: coupon check hits a thin /api/coupon/validate that wraps the pricing service with a 30s cache; data: the resolved discount is stored in form state and sent in the unchanged /api/checkout body; config: a LaunchDarkly flag checkout_schema_form gates new vs legacy per region; tests: schema unit tests + an async-validation integration test with a mocked slow pricing service; rollout: flag canary at 5% then ramp.

after_state.description: Schema-driven form; coupon validated live with debounce, dedupe, and cache; legacy contract preserved.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for field rules + async coupon refinement
Dedupe + 30s cache to protect pricing service under flash-sale load
Declarative form bound to schema with debounced live coupon check
Per-region kill switch between schema form and legacy

migration_strategy: Ship /api/coupon/validate wrapping the pricing service with a 30s cache; no UI change yet
Land schema form behind checkout_schema_form flag, default off; legacy renders when off
Canary flag at 5% in one low-volume region; watch coupon endpoint QPS and checkout error rate
Ramp 5->25->100% over a week, region by region, comparing late-failure rate vs legacy
Once a full sale passes clean, remove legacy CheckoutForm.jsx and the flag
Rollback at any step by flipping the flag off per region (instant)
testing_strategy: Unit: checkoutSchema.parse over fixtures (valid email/zip, malformed zip '123', empty address) asserting issue paths. Integration: render CheckoutForm with msw mocking /api/coupon/validate; type 'SAVE10' then 'BADCODE' and assert (output) inline error appears for bad code and (side-effect) only ONE network call fires per code due to dedupe/cache by inspecting the msw request log. Chaos: make the mocked pricing service hang 5s; assert the form stays interactive (other fields validate) and a timeout shows 'checking…' then a retriable error, and that /api/checkout is never POSTed while coupon is invalid.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The onBlur-fetch naive fix issues an uncached request per field-blur; at 40k concurrent shoppers during a flash sale the coupon/pricing service is overwhelmed (no dedupe, no cache), causing cascading 503s that block ALL checkouts — the exact failure schema+dedupe avoids.

title: Add async username-uniqueness validation to a Next.js signup form without race conditions
business_context: A B2B SaaS (devtools) signup form lets users pick a workspace slug. Today uniqueness is only enforced by a DB constraint, so users fill the whole 5-field form, submit, and get a 409 with a generic 'try again'. 8% of signups abandon at this step. Product wants live 'slug available?' checks like GitHub, but two users typing the same slug must never both pass the live check and then collide.
constraints: Live check must not falsely report 'available' for a slug a concurrent user is mid-claiming
DB unique constraint stays authoritative; live check is advisory only
Slug check endpoint must tolerate 2k checks/min without a full table scan
Reserved slugs (admin, api, www, ~200 words) must be rejected client+server
Works across 4 app server instances (no in-process-only state)
Submit must still succeed-or-fail atomically; no partial workspace rows

before_state.description: No live check; uniqueness surfaces as a late 409 with no field attribution.

issues: 409 not mapped to the slug field, just a generic alert
No live availability check -> late abandonment
Reserved slugs only fail at DB or not at all

reasoning: Naive approach: a GET /api/slug/available that just does SELECT 1 WHERE slug=$1 and returns available=true if no row. Rejected: this has a TOCTOU race — two users both see 'available', both submit, one gets a 500-shaped 409 we still can't attribute. Alternative considered: client-side-only blacklist + optimistic claim; rejected because it can't see other users' in-flight slugs. Chosen path: keep the DB unique index authoritative (data) and add a short-lived Redis soft-reservation so the live check is honest across instances (async/config). UI: Next form debounces the check and shows available/taken/reserved states; API: /api/slug/check returns 'taken' if a row exists OR a Redis key ws:claim:<slug> is held by another session; on real submit we INSERT and let the unique constraint be the final arbiter, mapping its error back to the slug field; tests: race test with two concurrent claimers; rollout: additive endpoint, then enable UI.

after_state.description: Debounced live check backed by Redis soft-reservations; DB constraint remains authoritative; 409 mapped to slug.

files.purpose: Cross-instance honest availability using DB row + Redis soft-claim
Atomic insert; DB unique index is final arbiter; structured 409
Debounced live UI feedback with explicit states

migration_strategy: Add reserved-words list + /api/slug/check endpoint; nothing consumes it yet
Add Redis soft-reservation to the existing POST /api/workspaces (no UI change), behind no flag since it's strictly safer
Ship SlugField behind a flag live_slug_check default off
Enable for internal workspaces, watch Redis key churn and check QPS
Ramp to 100%; keep the 409->field mapping permanently as the safety net
Rollback: disable flag (UI) and/or stop writing the Redis claim (POST still safe via unique index)
testing_strategy: Integration: spin two POST /api/workspaces with the same slug concurrently via Promise.all; assert exactly one 200 and one structured 409 {field:'slug'} and exactly one DB row (side-effect query). Unit: /api/slug/check returns 'reserved' for 'admin', 'invalid' for 'ab', 'taken' when a Redis claim exists. Chaos: kill Redis mid-test; assert /api/slug/check degrades to checking only the DB (returns 'available' when no row) and submit still works because the unique index is authoritative.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The SELECT-only live check creates a TOCTOU window: both concurrent users see 'available' and submit; without the Redis soft-claim the only thing stopping a double-create is the DB index, and without the P2002->field mapping the loser still gets an unattributed generic error — the abandonment we set out to fix.

title: Convert a fintech KYC wizard to a schema-driven multi-step form with cross-step conditional validation
business_context: A fintech onboarding KYC wizard (5 steps, ~60k applicants/month) hardcodes each step's validation in the component. Adding a new step or a conditional field (e.g. SSN required only for US residents) means editing 4 files and frequently breaks the 'back' button state. Compliance needs country-conditional rules and an audit log of which rule version validated each submission.
constraints: Conditional rules: SSN required iff country=US; tax ID format differs per country (US/UK/DE)
Going Back then Forward must not lose or silently revalidate-clear entered data
Each accepted submission must record the validation ruleset version for audit (immutable)
Schema changes must be backward compatible with in-flight applications (started under old ruleset)
PII (SSN, DOB) must never be logged in plaintext
All 5 steps' validation must run server-side too; client schema is advisory

before_state.description: Per-component imperative validation with brittle back/forward state and no rule versioning.

issues: Country-conditional logic duplicated across steps
Back button rebuilds local state and can drop ssn
No record of which rules validated the applicant
SSN flows through alert() and could be logged

reasoning: Naive approach: add more if/else per country inside each step component. Rejected: conditional matrix (countries x fields) explodes combinatorially across 5 components and is impossible to version for audit. Alternative: one giant flat Zod object validated only at the end; rejected because the wizard must validate per-step to gate Next, and a flat object can't express 'this step's subset'. Chosen path: a versioned ruleset module exporting per-step Zod schemas plus a discriminated/refined country branch (data layer); a wizard machine holds the full draft in one store so Back/Forward never loses data (UI/state); the chosen rulesetVersion is stamped onto the submission and persisted immutably (audit/config); server re-runs the same schema module (shared) so client is advisory; tests cover US-vs-UK branching and back/forward retention; rollout keeps old in-flight apps on their pinned version.

after_state.description: Versioned per-step schemas with a country branch; single draft store; audited ruleset version; shared server validation.

files.purpose: Versioned, country-branched per-step schemas reused by client and server
Single draft store so Back/Forward never loses data; per-step validation
Authoritative server validation + immutable ruleset-version audit + PII encryption

migration_strategy: Extract current rules into rules/v3.ts; have legacy components call it (no behavior change) to verify parity
Introduce useWizard store reading the same schemas, render new wizard behind flag kyc_schema_wizard off
Pin every in-flight application to the ruleset version it started with; new apps get v3
Canary new wizard for a single country (US) and compare drop-off + back-button bug reports
Ramp countries one at a time; keep server submitKyc validating regardless of which UI produced the draft
Rollback: flag off reverts UI; server validation and version stamping stay (strictly additive)
testing_strategy: Unit: stepSchemas('US').identity rejects 8-digit taxId, stepSchemas('UK') accepts 'AB123456C'; assert error path maps to 'taxId'. Component: enter identity, go Next then Back; assert (output) the SSN value persists and (side-effect) no revalidation cleared the store. Integration: submitKyc with valid US draft asserts a DB row with rulesetVersion='kyc-2026.06-v3' and that the persisted blob is ciphertext (negative: assert raw SSN string not present in the stored column). Chaos: submit with country switched US->UK mid-wizard; assert server rejects the now-wrong taxId format rather than trusting client state.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding more per-component if/else means the back button rebuilds Step2's local useState from props and silently drops the SSN when the user navigates back then forward; and without a stamped rulesetVersion, compliance cannot prove which rules approved an applicant — both are structural, not bugs you can patch in place.

title: Add optimistic UI with rollback to a Vue/Nuxt comment-edit form backed by a slow moderation API
business_context: A social platform's comment editor (Vue 3/Nuxt) waits for a moderation API (p95 900ms) before showing the edited comment, so the UI feels frozen and users double-submit. We want optimistic updates: show the edit instantly, but cleanly roll back and surface a field error if moderation rejects or the request fails — without losing the user's text.
constraints: Optimistic edit must roll back to the exact prior server value on rejection/failure
User's edited draft must survive a rollback so they can fix and retry
No duplicate edits if the user clicks Save twice (request must be idempotent)
Moderation verdict (approved/blocked/needs_review) maps to distinct UI states
Must work with the existing Pinia store shape; other components read the same comment
Network failure (offline) must be distinguishable from a moderation block

before_state.description: Blocking save; UI frozen during moderation; double-submits create duplicate edits.

files.content:
<script setup>
import { ref } from 'vue';
const props=defineProps(['comment']);
const text=ref(props.comment.body);
const saving=ref(false);
async function save(){
  saving.value=true;
  const r=await fetch(`/api/comments/${props.comment.id}`,{method:'PUT',body:JSON.stringify({body:text.value})});
  // UI only updates after the slow call returns
  props.comment.body=(await r.json()).body;
  saving.value=false;
}
</script>
<template><textarea v-model="text"/><button @click="save" :disabled="saving">Save</button></template>
issues: UI frozen ~900ms during moderation
Double click can fire two PUTs creating duplicate edits/audit rows
Mutates props directly, drifting from the Pinia store
No way to roll back if moderation blocks

reasoning: Naive approach: just set props.comment.body=text immediately then fire the request. Rejected: if moderation blocks or the network drops, the optimistic value is already committed to the shared store and other components show unmoderated text with no way back; also no idempotency means a double click creates two moderation rows. Alternative: a global toast on failure and reload the comment from server; rejected because a reload loses the user's edited draft and is jarring. Chosen path: store layer keeps the authoritative comment; the editor snapshots the prior value, applies an optimistic patch with an idempotency key, then on the moderation verdict either confirms or reverts to the snapshot while preserving the local draft (UI->store->async). API uses an Idempotency-Key header so a duplicate click is a no-op; UI distinguishes offline (catch) from blocked (verdict). Tests cover rollback-preserves-draft and idempotency; rollout is component-local behind a flag.

after_state.description: Optimistic patch with snapshot rollback, idempotency key, and distinct verdict/offline states; draft preserved.

files.purpose: Authoritative shared store with snapshot/optimistic/revert primitives
Optimistic apply, rollback-to-snapshot, draft retention, idempotent retry, verdict/offline split
Declarative UI mapping each verdict/offline state with draft preserved

migration_strategy: Add Idempotency-Key handling to PUT /api/comments/:id server-side (dedupe by key, 24h TTL) — backward compatible
Introduce Pinia snapshot/optimistic/revert actions; legacy editor untouched
Ship new CommentEditor behind flag optimistic_comment_edit off
Canary to 10% of editors; compare double-submit moderation rows and time-to-visible-edit
Ramp to 100%; delete the blocking save path
Rollback: flag off restores blocking editor; idempotency server change is safe to keep
testing_strategy: Component: mock PUT to return verdict:'blocked'; click Save; assert (output) status='blocked', the textarea still shows the user's draft, and (side-effect) store.byId[id].body equals the original snapshot (reverted). Idempotency: click Save twice rapidly; assert only one network PUT carries a new key and the server records one moderation row. Chaos: make fetch reject (simulated offline); assert status='offline', store reverted, draft retained, and a later retry reuses the same Idempotency-Key.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Immediately committing props.comment.body to the shared store means a moderation 'blocked' verdict leaves un-moderated text visible to every other component reading that comment, with no snapshot to revert to; and lacking an idempotency key, a double click creates two moderation/audit rows for one logical edit.

title: Introduce a JSON-Schema-driven dynamic form renderer for a healthcare intake with 200+ field variants
business_context: A healthcare provider builds intake forms per specialty (cardiology, pediatrics, etc.), each a slightly different set of ~40 fields. Currently every form is a bespoke React component (200+ across specialties), so a single validation-rule change (e.g., new BMI range) requires editing dozens of files. Clinicians want non-engineers to author forms, and every field's validation must run identically on the FHIR-backed server.
constraints: Form definitions authored as JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12) + UI hints, stored in DB, versioned
Same schema validates client-side and server-side (single source of truth)
Conditional fields (show 'pregnancy weeks' only if sex=F and pregnant=true) via JSON Schema if/then
Rendering 40 fields must stay under 100ms on a mid-range tablet
PHI fields must be flagged in schema and excluded from analytics events
Backward compat: existing bespoke forms keep working during migration

before_state.description: One bespoke component per specialty; rule changes require editing dozens of files; clinicians can't author.

issues: BMI rule duplicated across 200+ specialty components
Non-engineers cannot change a form
Conditional logic hand-coded and inconsistent
No shared client/server validation

reasoning: Naive approach: a shared validate() util imported by every bespoke component. Rejected: it removes some duplication but still requires an engineer to add fields and keeps 200 hand-written render trees; conditional logic stays bespoke. Alternative: a proprietary form-builder DSL; rejected because JSON Schema already standardizes validation, is supported by FHIR tooling, and lets the SAME document validate on the Node server via Ajv — no custom parser to maintain. Chosen path: store JSON Schema + uiHints per form version (data); a generic DynamicForm renders fields from the schema and validates with Ajv (UI); the server validates the submission with the identical compiled schema (shared); if/then encodes conditionals; phi:true flags exclude fields from analytics (config); tests verify a conditional schema and PHI exclusion; rollout runs new renderer alongside bespoke forms behind a per-specialty flag.

after_state.description: Generic Ajv-driven renderer; schemas authored/versioned in DB; identical client/server validation; PHI-aware analytics.

files.purpose: Single generic renderer validating any stored JSON Schema via Ajv
Authored, versioned schema with conditional requirement + PHI tags
Server validates with the identical schema and strips PHI from analytics

migration_strategy: Author the cardiology schema, validate it reproduces the existing component's rules against a fixture corpus
Add formSchema table (versioned) and the validateIntake server path; bespoke forms still live
Render DynamicForm for cardiology behind flag dynamic_intake[cardiology] off
Canary one clinic; compare submission validity and render time vs bespoke
Migrate specialties one at a time, retiring each bespoke component after parity
Rollback: per-specialty flag off restores the bespoke component instantly
testing_strategy: Unit: compile cardiology.v2.json; assert pregnancyWeeks becomes required only when sex='F' and pregnant=true (drive both branches), and bmi=70 fails. Integration: POST a submission missing pregnancyWeeks for a pregnant F patient; assert validateIntake returns ok:false and (side-effect) NO analytics event was emitted. PHI: submit valid data; assert the analytics event payload omits bmi and pregnancyWeeks. Chaos: feed a malformed/unknown schema version; assert validateIntake throws a typed error and never records a submission.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A shared validate() util still leaves 200 hand-written render trees and bespoke conditional logic, so a clinician cannot add a field and a rule change still touches dozens of components; and client-only validation drifts from the FHIR server because they aren't literally the same schema document.

title: Add cross-field date-range validation and async availability to a logistics booking form (FastAPI + React)
business_context: A freight logistics platform lets shippers book a pickup window. The React form validates each date in isolation, so users can pick an end-before-start range or a window with no available truck, only learning at submit. ~6% of bookings fail at submit, and ops re-contacts shippers. We need cross-field range rules plus an async 'is a truck available in this window?' check against a FastAPI capacity service.
constraints: End datetime must be after start; window must be >= 2h and <= 72h
Availability check hits the capacity service (p95 400ms) and must be debounced + cancelable
Stale availability responses (user changed dates again) must never overwrite newer results
Same range rules enforced server-side at booking creation (client advisory)
Handle 500 bookings/min peak without flooding capacity service
Timezone: dates are entered in the shipper's TZ but validated/stored in UTC

before_state.description: Per-field date validation; no cross-field range check; availability only known at submit; race-prone if added naively.

issues: No end-after-start or window-length validation
Availability only discovered at submit -> 6% failures
Dates sent without TZ normalization
No async availability hook at all

reasoning: Naive approach: add an end>start check and call the capacity API in an onChange. Rejected: onChange availability calls without cancellation create a race — a slow response for an old range can land after a newer one and show stale availability, plus 500 bookings/min would flood the service. Alternative: only validate range client-side and keep availability server-only; rejected because that doesn't remove the late-failure surprise we're paid to fix. Chosen path: a Zod schema with superRefine for the cross-field range (data/UI), a debounced availability query keyed by (startUTC,endUTC) with AbortController so stale responses are dropped (async), TZ conversion at the edge to UTC, identical range checks in the FastAPI create endpoint (server), and capacity service protected by debounce+cache. Tests cover range branches, stale-response dropping, and server parity; rollout is additive.

after_state.description: Cross-field range schema + cancelable debounced availability keyed to current range; UTC normalization; server parity.

files.purpose: Cross-field range rules as the single source of truth (UTC)
Debounced, cancelable availability that drops stale responses via reqId+AbortController
Authoritative server range+availability check mirroring client rules

migration_strategy: Add GET /api/capacity wrapping the capacity service with a 60s cache keyed by (start,end)
Land range validation server-side in create_booking (additive, stricter) and verify no valid current bookings regress
Ship the new form (schema + useAvailability) behind flag booking_async_avail off
Canary 5% of shippers; watch capacity QPS and submit-failure rate
Ramp to 100%; remove the old per-field validation path
Rollback: flag off restores old form; server range check is safe to keep
testing_strategy: Unit: bookingSchema rejects end<=start, a 1h window, and a 80h window with correct issue paths. Hook: fire check() for rangeA then rangeB before A resolves; resolve A last and assert (output) state reflects B, not A (stale dropped). Integration: POST a booking with no capacity; assert 409 'No truck available' and (side-effect) no DB row created. Chaos: capacity service times out; assert availability hook returns to 'idle' (not 'available') and submit still gets the authoritative server 409 if truly unavailable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: An onChange availability fetch without cancellation lets a slow response for an old date range resolve after a newer one, painting 'available' for dates the user already changed; the shipper books a window with no truck — re-introducing the exact submit-time failure, now hidden behind a misleading green check.

title: Refactor a Go/gRPC profile-update form to validate via shared protobuf-derived rules with field masks
business_context: A B2B SaaS user-profile editor (Go backend, gRPC) duplicates validation: the React client checks fields one way, the Go service another, and they drift, so ~3% of profile saves pass client but fail server. Partial updates use a FieldMask but server validates the whole message, rejecting valid partial edits. We want one rule set derived from protobuf-validate annotations and FieldMask-aware partial validation.
constraints: Only fields named in the FieldMask may be validated/updated; others untouched
Validation rules live in proto annotations (buf validate) shared by client and server
Email/phone format rules must match exactly client vs server
Concurrent edits to the same profile must use optimistic version (etag) to avoid lost updates
Backward compat: clients not sending a mask fall back to full-message validation
Audit: store which fields changed per update

before_state.description: Divergent client/server validation; server ignores FieldMask and rejects valid partial updates.

issues: Ignores UpdateMask: partial edits rejected for unrelated empty fields
Email rule (Contains @) differs from client regex -> drift
No optimistic concurrency: concurrent edits clobber
No record of changed fields

reasoning: Naive approach: copy the client regex into Go so they match. Rejected: two hand-maintained copies drift again on the next change; and it still ignores the FieldMask. Alternative: validate only on the server and strip client validation; rejected because users need instant inline feedback and a round-trip per keystroke is unacceptable. Chosen path: declare rules once as buf validate (protovalidate) annotations on the proto (data/source of truth); the Go server runs protovalidate but scoped to masked fields only (server); the client consumes the same generated descriptors via a small wasm/ts validator so rules are literally identical (UI); add an etag/version field for optimistic concurrency (data); record changed fields for audit (config). Tests assert mask scoping and client/server parity; rollout keeps full-message validation as the no-mask fallback.

after_state.description: Single protovalidate rule set; mask-scoped validation; optimistic version; change audit; client parity.

files.purpose: Rules declared once as proto annotations shared by both sides
Mask-scoped protovalidate + optimistic version + change audit
Client validation using the SAME protovalidate rules and mask scope

migration_strategy: Add buf validate annotations + regenerate; server keeps current logic but cross-checks against protovalidate (log mismatches)
Once mismatches are zero, switch server to protovalidate as authoritative (full-message mode)
Add UpdateMask scoping with no-mask full-validation fallback (backward compatible)
Add version field + optimistic check; clients start sending version
Ship client protovalidate; canary inline parity, then ramp
Rollback: server falls back to full-message validate; mask scoping is opt-in via sending a mask
testing_strategy: Unit (Go): UpdateProfile with mask=['display_name'] and an empty phone succeeds (phone not validated), while mask=['phone'] with 'abc' fails. Parity: a property test feeds 1k random profiles to both validateProfile (TS) and protovalidate (Go) and asserts identical violation field sets. Concurrency: two UpdateProfile calls with the same stale version; assert one Aborted 'version conflict' and (side-effect) store shows a single applied change. Chaos: send a mask path that doesn't exist; assert InvalidArgument, no write, and audit records nothing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Copying the regex into Go fixes today's drift but the server still validates the whole Profile, so a user editing only display_name is rejected because phone happens to be empty — a valid partial update fails, and the two hand-copied regexes drift again at the next change.

title: Add server-driven field-level validation errors to a NestJS + React payment-payout form
business_context: A fintech payout form (NestJS API, React) returns validation errors as a flat 400 'Invalid input' string, so the frontend can't attach errors to fields and shows a banner. Bank-account validation (IBAN checksum, routing-number async lookup) only happens server-side. Users retry blindly; ~9% of payouts need 2+ attempts. We want structured per-field server errors plus async IBAN/routing validation surfaced inline.
constraints: Server must return errors as {field, code, message}[] so the client maps to inputs
IBAN checksum (mod-97) validated client AND server; routing number requires async bank lookup (p95 500ms)
Async routing lookup must be rate-limited per user (10/min) to prevent enumeration
Backward compat: legacy mobile client expects the old flat error; negotiate via Accept-Version header
No bank credentials or full account numbers in logs
Idempotent payout creation keyed by client-provided requestId

before_state.description: Flat string errors; no field attribution; async bank checks only at submit; no rate limit.

issues: All failures collapse to 'Invalid input' -> no field mapping
Routing lookup unthrottled -> account enumeration risk
No idempotency: a retry can double-pay
Same IBAN check not shared with client

reasoning: Naive approach: change the message to include the field name in the string and parse it on the client. Rejected: string-parsing errors is fragile and breaks the moment a message is reworded or localized; it also doesn't add rate limiting or idempotency. Alternative: a global ValidationPipe with class-validator returning its default shape; rejected because async bank/routing checks can't live in class-validator decorators cleanly and the default shape differs from what mobile expects. Chosen path: a ValidationException filter emitting {field,code,message}[] (server) with a content-negotiated legacy fallback; a shared iban.ts (mod-97) used by client and server (UI/shared); an async routing validator behind a per-user rate limiter (async/config); idempotency by requestId (data). Client maps codes to inputs. Tests cover field mapping, rate limit, idempotency; rollout via Accept-Version.

after_state.description: Structured per-field errors with legacy negotiation; shared IBAN check; rate-limited async routing; idempotent create.

files.purpose: Single mod-97 IBAN check imported by client and server
Field-level errors, rate-limited async routing, idempotent create
Emit structured errors, with content-negotiated legacy flat fallback
Client uses same IBAN check and maps server field codes to inputs

migration_strategy: Ship the structured 422 + filter; default Accept-Version still returns legacy flat error so nothing breaks
Add the shared iban.ts and rate limiter (no behavior change for callers)
Web client opts into Accept-Version: 2 and maps fields; mobile stays on v1
Add idempotent createIdempotent keyed by requestId; web starts sending requestId
Migrate mobile to v2 over a release; then make 422 the default
Rollback: clients can drop Accept-Version to get legacy behavior; idempotency is safe to keep
testing_strategy: Integration: POST a bad IBAN with Accept-Version:2; assert 422 with errors:[{field:'iban',code:'IBAN_CHECKSUM'}] and (side-effect) no payout row. Legacy: same request with Accept-Version:1 returns 400 {error:'Invalid input'}. Rate limit: 11 routing lookups in a minute; assert the 11th returns a 429-mapped field error and the bank service was called <=10 times. Idempotency: POST same requestId twice; assert one payout row. Chaos: bank lookup throws; assert a routing field error, no payout, and no account number in logs (assert log scrubber).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Embedding the field name in the error string forces the client to regex-parse human messages; the moment the message is localized or reworded the mapping silently breaks and every error falls back to a banner again — and the naive path still leaves routing lookups unthrottled (enumeration) and payouts non-idempotent (double-pay on retry).

title: Add debounced async email-deliverability validation to a Django newsletter signup form
business_context: A media company's newsletter signup (Django, server-rendered with htmx) accepts any syntactically valid email, so 14% of addresses bounce, hurting sender reputation. They want async MX/deliverability validation as the user types, with graceful degradation when the verification provider is slow or down, and they must not block signups during provider outages.
constraints: Deliverability check calls an external verifier (p95 600ms, sometimes 5s); must time out at 1.2s
On verifier timeout/error, allow signup (fail-open) but mark the row 'unverified'
Debounce checks; never exceed the verifier's 100 req/s contract (token bucket)
Same email normalized (lowercase, gmail dot-stripping) client and server
Must work with htmx partial responses (no SPA)
Don't double-charge the verifier for an email already checked this session

before_state.description: Syntactic-only validation; high bounce rate; no async deliverability; verifier would block signups if added naively.

issues: Only checks for '@' -> 14% bounce
No deliverability/MX check
No normalization -> duplicate gmail variants
Would block signups if verifier is slow

reasoning: Naive approach: call the verifier synchronously in subscribe() and reject undeliverable emails. Rejected: the verifier occasionally takes 5s and sometimes goes down; a synchronous, fail-closed check would stall or block ALL signups during a provider outage — unacceptable for a growth form. Alternative: only verify in a nightly batch; rejected because it doesn't give the user live feedback to fix a typo'd domain. Chosen path: an htmx-driven debounced endpoint /subscribe/check that calls the verifier with a 1.2s timeout and a token bucket (async/config), normalizing the email first (shared util used by both check and subscribe). It fails open: on timeout/error the user can still submit and the Subscriber row is stored with status='unverified' for later batch recheck (data). A per-session cache avoids re-charging the verifier. Tests cover fail-open, normalization dedupe, rate limit; rollout is additive.

after_state.description: Debounced htmx deliverability check, fail-open with 'unverified' status, normalized+deduped, rate-limited.

files.content:
def normalize_email(raw:str)->str:
    e=raw.strip().lower()
    local,_,domain=e.partition('@')
    if domain in ('gmail.com','googlemail.com'):
        local=local.split('+')[0].replace('.','')
        domain='gmail.com'
    return f'{local}@{domain}'
files.purpose: Single normalization used by both check and subscribe (dedupe parity)
Live check + fail-open subscribe with normalized dedupe and status
Timeout + token-bucket guard so the verifier contract is respected

migration_strategy: Add normalize_email and apply it in subscribe() (dedupe), no verifier yet — backfill-safe
Add verifier.py with token bucket + 1.2s timeout; wire check_email htmx endpoint behind a template flag
Enable the live check widget; subscribe() starts recording status (verified/unverified/undeliverable)
Add a nightly batch job to recheck status='unverified' rows
Monitor bounce rate drop; if verifier costs spike, raise debounce / lower bucket
Rollback: remove the htmx check widget; subscribe() still works (fail-open keeps signups flowing)
testing_strategy: Unit: normalize_email('Foo.Bar+news@googlemail.com') == 'foobar@gmail.com'. View: post the same address twice; assert one Subscriber row (update_or_create). Fail-open: monkeypatch verify_deliverable to raise TimeoutError; assert subscribe still creates the row with status='unverified' and returns _ok. Rate limit: drain the bucket and assert verify_deliverable raises VerifierDown and check_email renders state='unknown' (not 'undeliverable'). Chaos: verifier returns 503; assert fail-open path and that bounce metric is not used to block signup.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A synchronous fail-closed verifier call ties every signup to the external provider's availability; when the provider has its periodic 5s slowdown or an outage, the form either hangs past the request timeout or rejects everyone — turning a deliverability nicety into a total signup outage during exactly the moments you can least afford it.

title: Build a typed form-state machine to coordinate dependent async validations in a gaming tournament-registration form
business_context: An esports platform's tournament registration form has interdependent async checks: team-name uniqueness, each of 5 roster members' eligibility (rank, region, not banned), and roster-size rules that change by tournament tier. Today these run as a tangle of useEffects, causing flicker, out-of-order results, and occasionally letting an ineligible roster through. Registration is open for 20-minute windows with thousands racing to register popular tournaments.
constraints: Roster eligibility depends on tournament tier loaded async first; checks must sequence correctly
Member eligibility checks (5x) run in parallel but their results must not interleave incorrectly
Team-name uniqueness must re-run if name changes after eligibility passed
During the 20-min window, thousands register; member-eligibility service must be batched (1 request for 5 members)
A late eligibility failure must block submit even if the button briefly showed enabled
Deterministic final gate: server re-validates the whole roster atomically

before_state.description: Tangle of useEffects with out-of-order async results and a submit button that can enable prematurely.

issues: Per-member fetches race; a late failure may not flip rosterOk back
Eligibility doesn't depend on the tier that must load first
Submit can enable before all checks resolve
5 separate requests per member hammer the service

reasoning: Naive approach: add more useEffects and a loading boolean. Rejected: independent effects can't express the dependency graph (tier -> roster rules -> member batch -> name) and out-of-order resolutions leave stale 'ok' flags; the submit button enables on a transient true. Alternative: chain everything sequentially with awaits in one effect; rejected because it serializes the 5 member checks (slow) and re-runs all work on any field change. Chosen path: model the form as an explicit state machine (XState) with states load_tier -> validating(parallel member batch + name) -> ready/blocked, where transitions carry the result so order is deterministic (UI/async). Member checks batch into one request; a name change after ready transitions back to validating. The server re-validates the full roster atomically as the deterministic gate (data). Tests assert ordering, batch count, and re-validation on name change; rollout behind a flag during a low-stakes tournament.

after_state.description: Explicit state machine sequencing tier->roster->name with batched member checks and an atomic server gate.

files.purpose: Deterministic sequencing of tier->parallel(roster,name)->ready with re-validation
One batched eligibility request for all 5 members, tier-aware
Atomic server gate re-validating the full roster + name in one transaction

migration_strategy: Add /api/eligibility/batch returning per-member results; keep single-member endpoint for now
Add the atomic registerTeam server transaction (authoritative gate) behind the existing endpoint
Introduce the XState machine behind flag reg_state_machine off; legacy form still default
Canary on a low-stakes tournament; verify no flicker and submit only enables when truly ready
Ramp to high-traffic tournaments; retire the useEffect form and single-member endpoint
Rollback: flag off restores legacy form; server batch + transaction are safe to keep
testing_strategy: Machine test (with mocked services): resolve member batch BEFORE tier resolves in one run and AFTER in another; assert in both cases the machine only reaches 'ready' once tier+roster+name all pass (deterministic). Batch: register 5 members; assert exactly ONE /api/eligibility/batch call, not five. Re-validation: reach 'ready', send NAME_CHANGED; assert it transitions back to 'validating' and nameOk resets. Integration: registerTeam with one ineligible member; assert 409 and (side-effect) no team row created. Chaos: make checkName resolve after a NAME_CHANGED for a newer name; assert the stale result is discarded by the machine's current state.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Independent useEffects can't encode that roster rules depend on the tournament tier, so member checks fire with undefined tier and may pass an ineligible roster; and because each effect owns its own boolean, an out-of-order late failure leaves rosterOk stuck true and the submit button enabled — exactly how ineligible teams slip into the bracket.

title: Add optimistic inline-edit with conflict detection to an IoT device-config form over MQTT/WebSocket
business_context: An IoT fleet console lets operators edit device config (sample rate, thresholds) inline. Today the form POSTs and waits for the device to ACK over MQTT (1-8s round trip on cellular), so the grid of 500 devices feels dead while editing. We want optimistic inline edits that reconcile against the device's reported config, detecting and surfacing conflicts when a device rejects a value or another operator edited it.
constraints: Device ACK over MQTT can take 1-8s; UI must update optimistically and reconcile on ACK/NACK
If the device reports a different value than the optimistic one (rejected/clamped), show a conflict
Concurrent operators: a second operator's committed change must surface as a conflict, not silent overwrite
Config writes are versioned; device only accepts a write if it carries the current version (CAS)
WebSocket reconnect must resync state without losing pending optimistic edits
Each device's pending edit is independent in a 500-row virtualized grid

before_state.description: Blocking write-then-wait; grid feels frozen; no conflict detection; last-write-wins overwrites peers.

issues: No optimistic update -> grid feels frozen during 1-8s ACK
No version/CAS -> concurrent operators overwrite each other
No reconcile when device clamps/rejects a value
WebSocket resync would clobber pending edits

reasoning: Naive approach: optimistically set the value and clear it when any ACK arrives. Rejected: without versioning, a NACK (device clamped the value) or a peer's concurrent edit is indistinguishable from success, so we silently show a wrong value; and on WS reconnect a full state resync would wipe pending edits. Alternative: lock the row until ACK; rejected because that re-introduces the frozen feel for 1-8s per edit. Chosen path: a per-device pending-edit store keyed by version; writes are compare-and-set (carry the version the operator saw) so the device/server reject stale writes with a conflict (data/async). The UI shows optimistic value + 'pending', then on ACK reconciles: match=commit, mismatch=conflict banner with device's actual value; NACK=conflict. WS reconnect merges server state with locally pending edits rather than replacing (UI). Tests cover NACK reconcile, concurrent CAS conflict, reconnect merge.

after_state.description: Per-device optimistic edits with CAS versioning, ACK/NACK reconciliation, conflict surfacing, reconnect-safe merge.

files.purpose: Per-device optimistic+reported state with reconcile-or-conflict on report
Compare-and-set write via If-Match; 409 signals a concurrent conflict
WS device-report stream + reconnect that re-snapshots and merges, not clobbers
Optimistic per-row UI showing pending/conflict with the device's actual value

migration_strategy: Add config version + If-Match CAS to PUT /api/devices/:id/config; old clients without If-Match still work (last-write-wins) temporarily
Add WS device-report stream carrying {config, nack, version}
Ship the optimistic store + DeviceRow behind flag fleet_optimistic off
Canary on a 20-device test fleet; verify ACK/NACK reconcile and peer-conflict surfacing
Ramp to full fleet; require If-Match (drop last-write-wins fallback)
Rollback: flag off restores blocking row; CAS server stays (clients can resend If-Match)
testing_strategy: Store test: startEdit then onReport with a clamped value (device returns sampleRate!=pending.value); assert status='conflict' and reported updated, pending NOT cleared. CAS: writeConfig with a stale baseVersion mock returns 409; assert the row shows a conflict with the current value (side-effect: store.pending status='conflict'). Reconnect: simulate ws close with a pending edit, then snapshot; assert pending edit survives the merge. Chaos: device NACKs (nack:true); assert conflict surfaced, not a silent commit; assert no second write auto-fires.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Clearing the optimistic value on any ACK treats a NACK (device clamped 1000ms to its max 500ms) and a peer's concurrent overwrite as success, so the operator sees their requested value while the device runs a different one; and a naive WS-reconnect full-resync wipes every in-progress optimistic edit across the 500-row grid.

title: Migrate a Formik e-commerce address form to schema-driven validation with async postal-code autofill
business_context: An e-commerce checkout uses Formik with a 300-line validate() function. Address errors are inconsistent across locales, and there's no postal-code autofill (users hand-type city/state). 7% of orders ship late due to address typos. We want a Zod schema per country, async postal-code lookup that autofills city/state, and consistent inline errors — while keeping the existing Formik-based parent untouched during migration.
constraints: Per-country schemas (US ZIP, CA postal, UK postcode) with country-conditional required fields
Postal-code lookup (p95 250ms) autofills city/state but user can override; override must not be re-clobbered
Lookup debounced and cached; failures degrade to manual entry
Must coexist with the legacy Formik form during migration (shared submit handler)
Address normalization (uppercase postal, trim) identical client and server
No layout shift when autofill populates fields

before_state.description: Giant Formik validate() with locale-inconsistent rules and no postal autofill.

files.content:
<Formik initialValues={{country:'US',zip:'',city:'',state:''}} validate={values=>{
  const e={};
  if(values.country==='US' && !/^\d{5}$/.test(values.zip)) e.zip='bad zip';
  // 300 more lines of per-country if/else, partly stale
  if(!values.city) e.city='required';
  return e;
}}>{/* fields */}</Formik>
issues: 300-line validate() with drifting per-country rules
No postal autofill -> typo'd city/state -> 7% late shipping
City always required even where lookup could fill it
Normalization inconsistent with server

reasoning: Naive approach: keep Formik but split validate() into per-country functions. Rejected: still hand-maintained, still no autofill, and the conditional-required matrix stays imperative. Alternative: rip out Formik entirely and rewrite the whole checkout in RHF; rejected as too large/risky for one sprint and it would touch unrelated payment fields. Chosen path: introduce per-country Zod schemas (data) and a small AddressFields subtree validated by Zod that plugs into the existing Formik form via a shared validate adapter (coexistence), plus a debounced cached postal-lookup that autofills city/state while respecting user overrides via a 'touched' guard (async/UI). Normalization is one shared util used client+server. Tests cover per-country rules, autofill-then-override, and degradation; rollout swaps only the address subtree behind a flag.

after_state.description: Per-country Zod schemas plugged into Formik via adapter; debounced cached postal autofill respecting overrides; shared normalization.

files.purpose: Per-country schemas + an adapter returning Formik-shaped errors
Debounced+cached autofill that never overwrites user-edited fields
Address subtree that autofills then tracks overrides; plugs into existing Formik

files.content: export function normalizePostal(raw:string){ return raw.trim().toUpperCase(); }
files.purpose: Single normalization shared by client form and server validation

migration_strategy: Add per-country Zod schemas + validateAddress adapter; wire Formik's validate to call it for address fields only
Add /api/postal lookup with cache; ship AddressFields subtree behind flag address_autofill off
Canary US only; verify autofill, override retention, and parity of error messages with legacy
Enable CA/UK schemas; compare late-shipping rate vs control
Remove the legacy address branch of validate() once parity holds
Rollback: flag off restores legacy address fields; schemas/adapter are inert when unused
testing_strategy: Unit: validateAddress('CA',{zip:'K1A0B1',...}) passes, 'CA' with '12345' fails on zip. Component: type a US ZIP; assert city/state autofill from mocked /api/postal; then edit city manually and re-type the same ZIP; assert city is NOT re-clobbered (override respected) — side-effect: overridden set contains 'city'. Degradation: mock /api/postal to 500; assert no autofill and city stays manually required. Chaos: rapid ZIP edits; assert only the debounced final lookup fires and a stale cached miss doesn't overwrite a newer typed city.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Splitting validate() into per-country functions still leaves autofill out, so users keep hand-typing city/state and the 7% typo-driven late-shipping persists; and any naive autofill that always writes city/state will clobber a user's deliberate correction the moment they re-touch the ZIP, producing wrong addresses that look 'verified'.

title: Add server-authoritative, schema-shared validation to a multi-tenant B2B form builder with per-tenant rules
business_context: A B2B SaaS form-builder lets each tenant define custom forms with custom validation (regex, ranges, required-if). Validation currently runs only in the browser, so API integrations and malicious users bypass it, polluting tenant databases with invalid records (one tenant had 40k bad rows). We need the SAME tenant-defined rules enforced server-side, compiled safely (no arbitrary regex DoS), and versioned so changing a rule doesn't retroactively invalidate stored submissions.
constraints: Tenant-defined regex must be sandboxed against catastrophic backtracking (ReDoS) with a timeout
Same rule definition validates browser and server (one engine, two runtimes)
Rule changes are versioned; historical submissions keep their original ruleset version
Multi-tenant isolation: tenant A's rules/compilation must never affect tenant B (no shared mutable cache key collision)
API submissions (no browser) must be validated server-side authoritatively
Validate 10k submissions/min across tenants without recompiling rules each time

before_state.description: Browser-only validation; API and malicious clients bypass it; unbounded tenant regex; no rule versioning.

issues: Server doesn't validate -> API/malicious clients insert invalid rows (40k bad)
new RegExp(tenant pattern) -> ReDoS risk, no timeout
No rule versioning -> editing a rule retroactively breaks history
Same engine not reused server-side

reasoning: Naive approach: call the same clientValidate on the server with Node's RegExp. Rejected: Node's RegExp has no timeout, so a tenant's catastrophic pattern on attacker input pins a CPU core (ReDoS), and a per-request new RegExp recompiles 10k/min. Alternative: ship a fixed allow-list of validators and forbid custom regex; rejected because custom regex is a core product feature tenants pay for. Chosen path: one validation engine module shared by client and server (shared); on the server, compile tenant regex with re2 (linear-time, no backtracking) behind a per-version compiled-rule cache keyed by tenantId+formId+version (config/perf) so isolation holds and we don't recompile; submissions store the rulesetVersion that validated them, frozen for history (data); the server is authoritative and rejects API submissions that fail. Tests cover ReDoS resistance, server rejection of bypass, version freezing, tenant isolation; rollout dual-runs validation (log-only) before enforcing.

after_state.description: Shared engine with re2 server compilation, per-version compiled cache, tenant isolation, frozen rule versions, authoritative API validation.

files.purpose: Single rule-evaluation engine used by both runtimes (regex injected as compiled)
ReDoS-safe (RE2) per-version compiled cache with tenant-isolated keys
Authoritative server validation + frozen rulesetVersion on each row
Client reuses the identical engine for instant feedback

migration_strategy: Extract the shared engine; client uses it (no behavior change) to prove parity with old clientValidate
Add server submit() in log-only mode: validate but still accept, recording would-be rejections
Once log-only shows expected reject rate, flip server to enforce (422) for API + browser
Add rulesetVersion to submissions and pin compiled cache by version; historical rows keep old version
Backfill: leave existing rows' version null = 'legacy'; only new rows pinned
Rollback: switch server back to log-only (accept) instantly; client validation unaffected
testing_strategy: Unit: evaluate with a requiredIf rule (field B required when A='yes') across both branches. ReDoS: compile pattern '(a+)+$' and test against 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaX'; assert RE2 returns quickly (bounded time) where Node RegExp would hang. Bypass: POST an invalid value directly to submit() (simulating an API client); assert 422 and (side-effect) NO submission row created. Versioning: submit under v2, then bump form to v3; assert the stored row's rulesetVersion stays 2. Isolation: compile tenant A and tenant B with same formId/version; assert distinct cache entries and that editing A's rules doesn't change B's compiled set.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Running the tenant's pattern through Node's RegExp on the server gives an attacker a one-line DoS: a crafted catastrophic pattern plus crafted input pins a CPU core per request; and without server enforcement, API integrations keep inserting invalid rows (the 40k-bad-row incident) because client-only validation is trivially bypassed.

title: Add field-level async fraud-score validation to a fintech card-application form with circuit breaking
business_context: A consumer-lending card application (Next.js + Node) currently runs a fraud score only at final submit. High-risk applicants complete the full 8-field form before rejection, wasting a fraud-vendor call and frustrating legit users with vague denials. We want an async risk pre-check on the email+phone+IP combo as those fields are completed, with a circuit breaker so a fraud-vendor outage can't take down the form.
constraints: Risk pre-check calls a fraud vendor (p95 700ms, $0.02/call) only once the email+phone are valid
Circuit breaker: after N vendor failures, open and skip pre-check (form still submittable, flagged for manual review)
Pre-check result must not leak the risk reason to the client (regulatory)
Debounce + dedupe so editing phone after email doesn't double-charge the vendor
Final submit still runs authoritative fraud check server-side
Backward compat: applications started before this ship still submit normally

before_state.description: Fraud check only at submit; full form filled before denial; no breaker; vendor outage blocks submit.

issues: Fraud check only at submit -> wasted vendor calls + late denial
Leaks the fraud score/reason to client
No circuit breaker -> vendor outage blocks all submits
No dedupe -> editing fields re-charges vendor

reasoning: Naive approach: call fraud.score in an onBlur for email and phone. Rejected: separate calls per field double-charge the $0.02 vendor and a vendor outage makes onBlur hang, and the score must never reach the client. Alternative: pre-check client-side with a heuristic; rejected because real fraud signals require the vendor and a client heuristic is trivially gamed. Chosen path: a server pre-check endpoint that runs once email+phone are both valid (debounced+deduped by a hash of the triple) and returns only a coarse {ok|review|block} with no score (data/regulatory); wrap the vendor in a circuit breaker so repeated failures open it and the form proceeds flagged for manual review (async/resilience); final submit re-runs the authoritative check server-side regardless (data). UI shows a neutral 'verifying eligibility'. Tests cover dedupe, breaker-open behavior, and no-leak; rollout additive.

after_state.description: Debounced/deduped async risk pre-check returning coarse verdicts, circuit-broken vendor, no score leak, authoritative submit.

files.purpose: Circuit breaker so vendor outages degrade gracefully instead of blocking
Deduped, debounced, breaker-protected pre-check emitting only coarse verdicts
Authoritative submit reusing the deduped verdict; manual-review flag; no leak
Debounced UI trigger after email+phone valid; shows only neutral states

migration_strategy: Wrap the existing fraud.score call in the CircuitBreaker (submit path) — pure resilience win, no UI change
Add the coarse /api/apply/precheck endpoint (no client yet) and verify verdict distribution vs raw scores
Ship the debounced RiskField pre-check behind flag apply_precheck off
Canary 10%; confirm vendor call count drops (dedupe) and no score leaks in client payloads
Ramp to 100%; keep authoritative submit check permanently
Rollback: flag off removes pre-check UI; submit still protected by breaker-wrapped check
testing_strategy: Unit: breaker.run trips open after 5 failures and returns fallback 'review' while open. Dedupe: call precheck twice with the same email+phone+ip; assert fraud.score invoked once. No-leak: assert the precheck and submit 403 responses contain no numeric score field (negative assertion). Breaker integration: force fraud.score to throw 6x; assert the 6th+ pre-check returns verdict 'review' and the app is created with riskFlag='manual_review' (side-effect), not blocked. Chaos: vendor returns 0.85 (block); assert submit 403 with generic message and (side-effect) no application row.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-field onBlur fraud calls double-charge the $0.02 vendor for every applicant and, worse, hang the form when the vendor degrades (no breaker), so a vendor incident becomes a full application outage; and returning the raw score for inline display leaks regulated risk reasoning to the very applicants you're scoring.

title: Replace alert-based validation in a Svelte contact form with reactive schema validation and aria errors
business_context: A SaaS marketing site's contact form (SvelteKit) uses alert() for validation errors, which is inaccessible (no aria), blocks the thread, and is unusable on mobile. Conversion analytics show 22% of mobile users abandon after the first alert. We want reactive inline validation with proper aria-invalid/aria-describedby and an async 'is this a known spam domain' check, all server-validated too.
constraints: Errors must be accessible: aria-invalid + aria-describedby pointing at a live error region
Spam-domain check is async (p95 150ms) and must not block typing other fields
Same validation runs in the SvelteKit form action server-side (progressive enhancement: works without JS)
No alert(); errors render inline and announce to screen readers (role=alert)
Honeypot + async spam check together; legit users never see the honeypot
Submission still works with JavaScript disabled (server validates and re-renders)

before_state.description: alert()-based validation: inaccessible, blocking, mobile-hostile; no async spam check; no progressive enhancement.

files.content:
<script>
  let email=''; let message='';
  function submit(){
    if(!email.includes('@')){ alert('bad email'); return; }
    if(message.length<10){ alert('message too short'); return; }
    fetch('/contact',{method:'POST',body:JSON.stringify({email,message})});
  }
</script>
<input bind:value={email}/><textarea bind:value={message}/><button on:click={submit}>Send</button>
issues: alert() is inaccessible and blocks the thread
22% mobile abandon on first alert
No async spam-domain check
Breaks entirely with JS disabled

reasoning: Naive approach: replace alert() with a single error <div> at the top. Rejected: a top-of-form error blob isn't associated with the offending input (no aria-describedby), so screen readers and mobile users still can't tell which field failed, and it still has no async spam check or no-JS path. Alternative: client-only inline errors; rejected because it abandons progressive enhancement and lets JS-disabled/bot submissions through unvalidated. Chosen path: a Zod schema shared by the Svelte component and the SvelteKit form action (shared/server); per-field reactive errors wired with aria-invalid + aria-describedby + role=alert (UI/a11y); an async spam-domain check debounced on email blur that never blocks other fields (async); a honeypot field hidden from assistive tech; the form action re-validates and returns field errors so it works without JS (progressive enhancement). Tests cover aria wiring, async non-blocking, no-JS server validation.

after_state.description: Reactive accessible inline validation, shared Zod schema, async spam check, progressive enhancement via form action.

files.purpose: Shared schema (incl. honeypot) for client and server
Server form action: authoritative validation + spam + honeypot; works without JS

files.content: {errors.email || spamMsg || (form?.errors?.email ?? '')}
files.purpose: Accessible reactive inline errors + async spam check + honeypot + works without JS

migration_strategy: Add contactSchema and the +page.server.ts action that validates server-side (covers no-JS immediately)
Replace alert() with reactive inline errors bound via aria-describedby/role=alert
Add the debounced async spam-domain check on email blur
Add the visually-hidden honeypot field + server honeypot rejection
A/B the new form vs old on mobile; measure abandonment
Rollback: server action and schema are safe to keep; revert the component to the previous version if needed
testing_strategy: Component (Testing Library): submit empty; assert the email input has aria-invalid='true' and its aria-describedby region (role=alert) contains the email message. Async non-block: blur email with a spam domain (mocked); assert the message textarea is still editable while the check runs. No-JS: invoke the form action directly with invalid data; assert fail(422) with field errors (side-effect: no db.contact row). Honeypot: action with company filled returns 400 and creates no row. Chaos: spam check endpoint errors; assert the email field stays usable and submit still proceeds to server validation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single top-of-form error <div> isn't programmatically tied to the failing input, so screen-reader and mobile users still can't tell what to fix — the accessibility and abandonment problem persists; and a client-only rewrite drops the no-JS path, letting bots and JS-disabled clients POST straight past validation.

title: Add optimistic multi-row inline editing with batched validation to a logistics rate-table grid
business_context: A logistics ops team edits shipping-rate tables (up to 2,000 rows: origin, dest, weight-band, price) in a grid. Today each cell edit POSTs synchronously and re-validates the whole table server-side (2-4s), so bulk edits are painfully slow and partial failures leave the grid inconsistent. We want optimistic per-cell edits, batched validation/save, and clear surfacing of which rows failed cross-row rules (e.g., no overlapping weight bands).
constraints: Cross-row rule: weight bands for a given origin/dest pair must not overlap (validated as a set)
Optimistic edits applied immediately; a failed batch must roll back only the failed rows
Batch save coalesces many cell edits into one request (debounced 800ms or on blur)
Concurrent editors: a save must be rejected if the table version changed (optimistic lock)
2,000-row grid must stay responsive (virtualized; no full re-validate per keystroke)
Server is authoritative for overlap rule; client check is a fast pre-filter

before_state.description: Per-cell synchronous save with whole-table revalidation; slow; partial failures corrupt grid state.

issues: Each cell edit revalidates the entire table (2-4s)
No batching -> bulk edits crawl
Partial failure leaves grid inconsistent with server
No overlap (cross-row) validation client-side

reasoning: Naive approach: keep per-cell PATCH but only revalidate the affected origin/dest group. Rejected: still one request per cell, still no optimistic feel for bulk edits, and concurrent editors can clobber via last-write-wins. Alternative: client-only validation and a single big save at the end; rejected because the overlap rule must be server-authoritative (rates affect billing) and a giant end-save loses partial progress. Chosen path: apply edits optimistically to a local model; a fast client-side overlap pre-check flags obvious problems instantly (UI); edits coalesce into a debounced batch PATCH carrying the table version for an optimistic lock (async/data); the server validates the overlap rule per affected group and returns per-row results so only failed rows roll back (data). Virtualization keeps the grid fast. Tests cover overlap, partial rollback, version conflict; rollout behind a flag.

after_state.description: Optimistic edits + fast client overlap pre-check + debounced batched versioned save + per-row rollback.

files.purpose: Shared O(n log n) weight-band overlap detector (client pre-check + server authority)
Optimistic edits, instant overlap pre-check, debounced versioned batch save, per-row rollback
Authoritative batched validation with per-row rollback and optimistic table lock

migration_strategy: Add /api/rates/batch PATCH (versioned, per-row results) alongside the per-cell endpoint
Ship the optimistic grid (useRateEdits) behind flag rate_grid_batch off
Canary one ops team; verify bulk-edit speed and that only overlapping rows roll back
Add the optimistic table version lock; reject stale batches with 409 + reload prompt
Ramp to all teams; deprecate the per-cell PATCH
Rollback: flag off restores per-cell editing; batch endpoint stays inert
testing_strategy: Unit: findOverlaps returns both rowIds for two overlapping bands on the same origin/dest, none for adjacent non-overlapping bands. Integration: PATCH a batch where one edit creates an overlap; assert the response marks that row ok:false, (side-effect) that row is rolled back to its previous value in the DB while the other edits persist, and the table version bumped once. Concurrency: two batches with the same If-Match; assert the second gets 409 and made no writes. Chaos: kill the DB mid-transaction; assert the whole batch rolls back atomically (no partial table) and the client keeps its dirty set for retry.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Revalidating only the affected group per cell still issues one round trip per keystroke across a 2,000-row bulk edit (minutes of waiting), and with last-write-wins two ops editing adjacent bands silently overwrite each other — producing overlapping rate bands that mis-bill customers, the exact rule the table exists to enforce.

title: Introduce schema-driven validation with async inventory check to a Shopify-style variant-picker form
business_context: An e-commerce product page lets shoppers pick variant options (size, color, material) that resolve to a SKU. The current form lets users pick out-of-stock combinations and only fails at add-to-cart, costing ~5% of add-to-cart attempts and frustrating shoppers. We want the variant form to disable impossible combos and async-check live inventory for the resolved SKU before enabling add-to-cart, at flash-sale scale.
constraints: Resolve option-combo -> SKU; disable combos that don't map to any SKU (combinatorial matrix)
Live inventory check per resolved SKU (p95 120ms) debounced; cached 10s; deduped
At flash-sale scale (30k concurrent), inventory service must not be flooded (cache + dedupe)
Stock can change between check and add-to-cart; add-to-cart server is authoritative (reserve or fail)
Backward compat: SSR product page must render a sane default (first in-stock SKU)
Selecting an option must immediately constrain the remaining options to in-stock paths

before_state.description: Free option selection with no SKU/stock awareness; out-of-stock combos selectable; failure only at cart.

issues: Lets users pick combos that map to no SKU or are out of stock
Stock only checked at add-to-cart -> 5% failure + alert
No combinatorial constraint as options are picked
No SSR-friendly default

reasoning: Naive approach: after each selection, call the inventory API for the resolved SKU. Rejected: at 30k concurrent during a flash sale this floods the inventory service (no cache/dedupe), and it still lets impossible combos be selected before resolving. Alternative: precompute all stock states server-side and ship them inert; rejected because stock changes mid-sale and a static snapshot quickly lies. Chosen path: a variant matrix (data) maps option combos to SKUs and the SSR page seeds an initial stock map; selecting an option constrains remaining options to combos that map to a SKU (UI); the resolved SKU's live stock is checked via a debounced, 10s-cached, deduped query (async/perf); add-to-cart is authoritative — it reserves stock or fails, since stock can move between check and click (data). Tests cover combo constraint, dedupe/cache, and reserve-or-fail; rollout additive with SSR default.

after_state.description: Variant matrix constrains combos; debounced/cached/deduped live stock; authoritative reserve-on-add; SSR default.

files.purpose: Combo->SKU resolver + per-option constraint so impossible combos are unselectable
Debounced + 10s-cached + deduped stock lookup to survive flash-sale load
Constrained option selects + debounced stock-gated add button + SSR default
Authoritative reserve-or-fail handling stock moving between check and click

migration_strategy: Build the variant matrix from existing product data; verify resolve() matches current resolveSku for all combos
Add /api/inventory/:sku (cacheable) and the authoritative reserve-on-add endpoint
Ship the constrained VariantPicker behind flag variant_stock_gate off; SSR seeds defaultSku
Canary a high-traffic product; watch inventory QPS (should be near-flat due to cache/dedupe) and add-to-cart failure rate
Ramp to catalog-wide; retire the old picker
Rollback: flag off restores free-selection picker; reserve endpoint stays (safer regardless)
testing_strategy: Unit: allowedFor('color',{size:'XL'}) returns only colors with an XL SKU; resolve returns null for a combo with no SKU. Cache/dedupe: select a SKU, then re-select the same SKU within 10s; assert one /api/inventory call. Integration: reserve succeeds then a second concurrent reserve for the last unit fails with 409 (side-effect: inventory decremented exactly once). Chaos: stock reported >0 by the cached check but inventory.reserve fails (sold out between check and click); assert add-to-cart returns 409 and the UI shows out-of-stock without adding to cart.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Calling the inventory API on every selection change floods the service during a 30k-concurrent flash sale (no cache/dedupe), and even when it works, a green 'Add to cart' computed from a stale read still 500s at the cart because nothing reserved the unit — the naive flow can't make stock both fast-to-display and correct-at-purchase.

title: Refactor a Kafka-backed bulk-import form to schema-validate each row and report errors per row asynchronously
business_context: A B2B SaaS lets customers upload CSVs (up to 500k rows) of contacts. Today the upload endpoint validates synchronously and rejects the entire file on the first bad row, so a single typo fails a 4-hour import. Customers re-upload repeatedly. We want per-row schema validation streamed through Kafka, with valid rows imported and a downloadable per-row error report, while the UI shows live progress.
constraints: Validate per row; valid rows import even if some rows fail (no all-or-nothing)
500k-row files must not be held in memory; stream/chunk through Kafka
Same field schema validates at upload-parse time and at the consumer (defense in depth)
Live progress to the UI (processed/valid/invalid counts) via SSE or WebSocket
Idempotent: re-processing the same upload (retry/replay) must not duplicate imported rows
Per-row errors collected into a downloadable report keyed by line number

before_state.description: Synchronous whole-file validation; first bad row fails the entire import; memory-heavy; no progress.

issues: First bad row aborts the whole 500k import
Entire file loaded into memory
No per-row error report or progress
Not idempotent on retry

reasoning: Naive approach: collect errors into an array and skip bad rows, still synchronous. Rejected: a 500k-row synchronous parse+insert holds the file in memory and blocks the request for hours, timing out the HTTP connection. Alternative: background job that processes the whole file in one worker; rejected because it doesn't parallelize and a worker crash restarts from zero. Chosen path: the upload endpoint streams the CSV in chunks, validating each row against a shared Zod schema and producing a Kafka message per row to an import.rows topic (data/async); consumers re-validate (defense in depth), insert valid rows idempotently keyed by uploadId+lineNumber, and emit per-row results to import.results; a results aggregator tallies progress (pushed to UI via SSE) and assembles an error report (config/UI). Idempotency via the (uploadId,line) key makes replay safe. Tests cover partial-success, idempotent replay, and consumer re-validation.

after_state.description: Streamed per-row Kafka validation; partial success; idempotent insert; live progress; downloadable error report.

files.purpose: Shared per-row schema used at upload-parse and at the consumer
Streams CSV rows into Kafka without buffering the whole file; idempotent uploadId
Per-row consumer: re-validate, idempotent upsert, emit result
Tally progress, build per-line error report, push live SSE to UI

migration_strategy: Stand up import.rows/import.results topics and the consumer/aggregator (no UI change)
Switch upload() to stream+produce behind flag streaming_import off; legacy sync path default
Canary with a customer's large file in a sandbox; verify partial-success and error report
Add SSE progress to the UI; enable for a few customers
Ramp to all; remove the synchronous whole-file path
Rollback: flag off restores sync upload (small files still work); topics stay idle
testing_strategy: Unit: validateRow rejects 'not-an-email' with a field-tagged error. Integration: feed a 1k-row fixture with 7 bad rows; assert 993 contacts upserted, the error report lists exactly those 7 line numbers, and upload.status becomes 'done'. Idempotency: replay the same import.rows messages; assert contact count unchanged (upsert by key). Defense in depth: send a message whose raw bypassed upload validation; assert the consumer still rejects it (no bad insert). Chaos: kill the consumer mid-stream and restart; assert it resumes from the offset and final counts match total with no duplicates.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Even collecting errors into an array, a synchronous 500k-row parse-and-insert buffers the whole file in memory and holds the HTTP request open for hours, so the load balancer times out and the customer never gets a result; and a single-worker background job has no idempotency, so a mid-import crash replays inserts and duplicates contacts.

title: Add password-policy + breach-check (k-anonymity) async validation to a Next.js account-creation form
business_context: A consumer app's signup accepts any 8+ char password. Security wants a configurable policy (length, classes, no common passwords) AND a Have-I-Been-Pwned breach check, surfaced inline as the user types — without ever sending the full password or its hash to a third party, and without blocking signup if the breach API is down.
constraints: Breach check must use k-anonymity (send only the first 5 chars of the SHA-1 hash, never the password)
Policy is server-configurable (length/classes/banlist) and the SAME policy validates server-side
Breach API (p95 300ms) failures fail-open with a soft warning, never block signup
Strength feedback debounced; no request per keystroke (debounce + only on pause)
Banned-password list (10k entries) checked client-side via a bloom filter to avoid shipping 10k strings
Password never logged; breach prefix request must not be attributable to a user

before_state.description: Minimal 8-char check; no policy, no breach check; would leak password if added naively.

issues: Only length>=8; no character classes or banlist
No breach check at all
A naive breach check might send the full password/hash
No shared server policy

reasoning: Naive approach: hash the password and POST it to HIBP's API. Rejected: sending even the full SHA-1 hash leaks which exact password the user chose to a third party; HIBP's k-anonymity API exists precisely to avoid this. Alternative: ship the 10k banned-passwords list to the client for instant checks; rejected because that's ~150KB and updates poorly. Chosen path: a shared policy module (config) drives both client strength feedback and the authoritative server check; the breach check uses k-anonymity — client computes SHA-1, sends only the 5-char prefix, and matches the suffix locally (async/privacy); a bloom filter (small) gives instant banlist hints client-side with the authoritative banlist on the server; breach failures fail-open with a soft warning (resilience). Passwords never logged. Tests cover k-anonymity, fail-open, policy parity, bloom false-positive handling.

after_state.description: Shared configurable policy, k-anonymity breach check (prefix-only), bloom-filter banlist hint, fail-open, server-authoritative.

files.purpose: Single configurable policy evaluator for client hints and server enforcement
k-anonymity breach lookup: never sends the password or full hash
Debounced inline policy + bloom hint + k-anonymity breach with fail-open
Server-authoritative policy + threshold breach gate, fail-open, no logging

migration_strategy: Add the shared policy module and enforce it server-side first (authoritative), with a lax default policy
Ship client strength hints (evaluatePolicy + bloom) — UX only, no enforcement change
Add the k-anonymity breach check client-side (soft warning) and server-side (hard threshold)
Tighten policy config (length/classes) gradually, watching signup completion
Roll banlist updates server-side; rebuild bloom periodically
Rollback: relax policy config instantly; breach check fails open so removing it is safe
